name: generalization error
best_quote: Test error, also referred to as generalization error, is the prediction error over an independent test sample

name: deviance
best_quote: The quantity −2 × the log-likelihood is sometimes referred to as the deviance.

name: Bayes factor
best_quote: is called the Bayes factor, the contribution of the data toward the posterior odds.

name: leave-one-out cross-validation
best_quote: The case K = N is known as leave-one-out cross-validation. In this case κ(i) = i, and for the ith observation the fit is computed using all the data except the ith.

name: leave-one-out
best_quote: 10-fold and N-fold cross-validation, the latter also known as leave-one-out (LOO).

name: test error
best_quote: Test error, also referred to as generalization error, is the prediction error over an independent test sample

name: generalized cross-validation
best_quote: Generalized cross-validation provides a convenient approximation to leave-one out cross-validation, for linear fitting under squared-error loss. The GCV approximation is GCV = (1/N)Σᵢ[yᵢ - f̂(xᵢ)]²/(1 - trace(S)/N)²

name: estimation bias
best_quote: The second term is the average squared estimation bias, the error between the average estimate E(x₀ᵀβ̂) and the best-fitting linear approximation. For linear models fit by ordinary least squares, the estimation bias is zero. For restricted fits, such as ridge regression, it is positive

name: akaike information criterion
best_quote: The Akaike information criterion is a similar but more generally applicable estimate of Err_in when a log-likelihood loss function is used. It relies on a relationship similar to (7.24) that holds asymptotically as N→∞

name: bayesian information criterion
best_quote: The Bayesian information criterion (BIC), like AIC, is applicable in settings where the fitting is carried out by maximization of a log-likelihood. The generic form of BIC is BIC = -2·loglik + log(N)·d

name: laplace approximation
best_quote: A so-called Laplace approximation to the integral followed by some other simplifications (Ripley, 1996, page 64) to (7.37) gives log Pr(Z|Mₘ) = log Pr(Z|θ̂ₘ, Mₘ) - (dₘ/2)log N

name: asymptotic consistency
best_quote: BIC is asymptotically consistent as a selection criterion. What this means is that given a family of models, including the true model, the probability that BIC will select the correct model approaches one as the sample size N→∞. This is not the case for AIC

name: minimum description length
best_quote: The minimum description length (MDL) approach gives a selection criterion formally identical to the BIC approach, but is motivated from an optimal coding viewpoint. The MDL principle says that we should choose the model that minimizes message_length = -log Pr(y|θ,M,X) - log Pr(θ|M)

name: shannon entropy
best_quote: A famous theorem due to Shannon says we should use code lengths lᵢ = -log₂ Pr(zᵢ) and the average message length satisfies average_length ≥ Σᵢ Pr(zᵢ)log₂ Pr(zᵢ). The right-hand side above is also called the entropy of the distribution

name: huffman coding
best_quote: In general the lower bound cannot be achieved, but procedures like the Huffmann coding scheme can get close to the bound.

name: leave-one-out bootstrap
best_quote: By mimicking cross-validation, a better bootstrap estimate can be obtained. For each observation, we only keep track of predictions from bootstrap samples not containing that observation. The leave-one-out bootstrap estimate of prediction error is defined by averaging over bootstrap samples C−i that do not contain observation i.

name: nonparametric bootstrap
best_quote: The bootstrap method described above, in which we sample with replacement from the training data, is called the nonparametric bootstrap. This really means that the method is 'model-free,' since it uses the raw data, not a specific parametric model, to generate new datasets.

name: log-likelihood
best_quote: This expression is called the log-likelihood, and each value ℓ(θ; zi) = log gθ(zi) is called a log-likelihood component.

name: data augmentation
best_quote: These problems are ones for which maximization of the likelihood is difficult, but made easier by enlarging the sample with latent (unobserved) data. This is called data augmentation. Here the latent data are the model memberships Δi.

name: GEM (generalized EM) algorithms
best_quote: This argument also makes it clear that a full maximization in the M step is not necessary: we need only to find a value θ̂(j+1) so that Q(θ', θ̂(j)) increases as a function of the first argument, that is, Q(θ̂(j+1), θ̂(j)) > Q(θ̂(j), θ̂(j)). Such procedures are called GEM (generalized EM) algorithms.

name: Algorithm 8.3 Gibbs Sampler
best_quote: For k = 1, 2, . . . , K generate U (t)ₖ from Pr(U (t)ₖ |U (t)₁, . . . , U (t)ₖ₋₁, U (t−1)ₖ₊₁, . . . , U (t−1)ₖ).

name: Algorithm 8.4 Gibbs sampling for mixtures
best_quote: (a) For i = 1, 2, . . . , N generate Δ(t)ᵢ ∈{0, 1} with Pr(Δ(t)ᵢ = 1) = γ̂ᵢ(θ(t)), from equation (8.42).

name: MM algorithms
best_quote: There are analogous definitions for majorization, for minimizing a function f(x). The resulting algorithms are known as MM algorithms, for 'Minorize-Maximize' or 'Majorize-Minimize.'

name: posterior distribution
best_quote: In the Bayesian approach to inference, we specify a sampling model Pr(Z|θ) (density or probability mass function) for our data given the parameters, and a prior distribution for the parameters Pr(θ) reflecting our knowledge about θ before we see the data. We then compute the posterior distribution Pr(θ|Z) = Pr(Z|θ)·Pr(θ) / ∫Pr(Z|θ)·Pr(θ)dθ.

name: working response
best_quote: This involves repeatedly fitting a weighted linear regression of a working response variable on the covariates; each regression yields a new value of the parameter estimates, which in turn give new working responses and weights.

name: .632 estimator
best_quote: The '.632 estimator' is designed to alleviate this bias. It is defined by 0.368 × err + 0.632 × 0Err(1). The derivation of the .632 estimator is complex; intuitively it pulls the leave-one out bootstrap estimate down toward the training error rate, and hence reduces its upward bias.

name: .632+ estimator
best_quote: Using this, the relative overfitting rate is defined to be a quantity that ranges from 0 if there is no overfitting (0Err(1) = err) to 1 if the overfitting equals the no-information value γ̂−err. Finally, we define the '.632+' estimator by The weight w ranges from .632 if R̂ = 0 to 1 if R̂ = 1, so 0Err(.632+) ranges from 0Err(.632) to 0Err(1).

name: no-information error rate
best_quote: First we define γ to be the no-information error rate: this is the error rate of our prediction rule if the inputs and class labels were independent. An estimate of γ is obtained by evaluating the prediction rule on all possible combinations of targets yi and predictors xi'.

name: relative overfitting rate
best_quote: Using this, the relative overfitting rate is defined to be a quantity that ranges from 0 if there is no overfitting (0Err(1) = err) to 1 if the overfitting equals the no-information value γ̂−err.

name: parametric bootstrap
best_quote: Consider a variation of the bootstrap, called the parametric bootstrap, in which we simulate new responses by adding Gaussian noise to predicted values: y∗i = μ̂(xi) + ε∗i; ε∗i ∼N(0, σ̂²); i = 1, 2, . . . , N.

name: likelihood function
best_quote: Maximum likelihood is based on the likelihood function, given by L(θ; Z) = ∏ᴺᵢ₌₁ gθ(zi), the probability of the observed data under the model gθ.

name: score function
best_quote: The score function is defined by ℓ̇(θ; Z) = ∑ᴺᵢ₌₁ ℓ̇(θ; zi) where ℓ̇(θ; zi) = ∂ℓ(θ; zi)/∂θ. Assuming that the likelihood takes its maximum in the interior of the parameter space, ℓ̇(θ̂; Z) = 0.

name: information matrix
best_quote: The information matrix is I(θ) = −∂²ℓ(θ; Z)/∂θ∂θᵀ. When I(θ) is evaluated at θ = θ̂, it is often called the observed information.

name: observed information
best_quote: When I(θ) is evaluated at θ = θ̂, it is often called the observed information.

name: fisher information
best_quote: The Fisher information (or expected information) is i(θ) = Eθ₀[I(θ)].

name: noninformative prior
best_quote: The distribution (8.25) with τ→∞ is called a noninformative prior for θ. In Gaussian models, maximum likelihood and parametric bootstrap analyses tend to agree with Bayesian analyses that use a noninformative prior for the free parameters.

name: predictive distribution
best_quote: The posterior distribution also provides the basis for predicting the values of a future observation znew, via the predictive distribution: Pr(znew|Z) = ∫Pr(znew|θ)Pr(θ|Z)dθ.

name: gaussian process prior
best_quote: Distributions on functions are fairly complex entities: one approach is to use a Gaussian process prior in which we specify the prior covariance between any two function values μ(x) and μ(x').

name: covariance kernel
best_quote: The implicit process prior for μ(x) is hence Gaussian, with covariance kernel K(x, x') = cov[μ(x), μ(x')] = τ·h(x)ᵀΣh(x').

name: expectation-maximization algorithm
best_quote: The EM algorithm is a popular tool for simplifying difficult maximum likelihood problems. These problems are ones for which maximization of the likelihood is difficult, but made easier by enlarging the sample with latent (unobserved) data. This is called data augmentation.

name: latent data
best_quote: These problems are ones for which maximization of the likelihood is difficult, but made easier by enlarging the sample with latent (unobserved) data. This is called data augmentation. Here the latent data are the model memberships Δi.

name: complete data log-likelihood
best_quote: Our observed data is Z, having log-likelihood ℓ(θ; Z) depending on parameters θ. The latent or missing data is Zm, so that the complete data is T = (Z, Zm) with log-likelihood ℓ0(θ; T), ℓ0 based on the complete density.

name: generalized em algorithms
best_quote: This argument also makes it clear that a full maximization in the M step is not necessary: we need only to find a value θ̂(j+1) so that Q(θ', θ̂(j)) increases as a function of the first argument, that is, Q(θ̂(j+1), θ̂(j)) > Q(θ̂(j), θ̂(j)). Such procedures are called GEM (generalized EM) algorithms.

name: markov chain monte carlo
best_quote: Having defined a Bayesian model, one would like to draw samples from the resulting posterior distribution, in order to make inferences about the parameters. In this section we discuss the Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) approach to posterior sampling.

name: gibbs sampling
best_quote: The Gibbs sampling procedure alternatively simulates from each of these distributions and when the process stabilizes, provides a sample from the desired joint distribution. Under regularity conditions it can be shown that this procedure eventually stabilizes, and the resulting random variables are indeed a sample from the joint distribution.

name: stationary distribution
best_quote: More formally, Gibbs sampling produces a Markov chain whose stationary distribution is the true joint distribution, and hence the term 'Markov chain Monte Carlo.'

name: burn-in period
best_quote: Here we have averaged over the last M−m+1 members of the sequence, to allow for an initial 'burn-in' period before stationarity is reached.

name: bagging
best_quote: Bootstrap aggregation or bagging averages this prediction over a collection of bootstrap samples, thereby reducing its variance. For each bootstrap sample Z*b, b = 1, 2, . . . , B, we fit our model, giving prediction f̂*b(x). The bagging estimate is defined by f̂bag(x) = (1/B)∑ᴮb=1 f̂*b(x).

name: empirical distribution
best_quote: Denote by P̂ the empirical distribution putting equal probability 1/N on each of the data points (xi, yi). In fact the 'true' bagging estimate is defined by EP̂f̂*(x), where Z* consists of observations sampled from P̂.

name: consensus vote
best_quote: For classification we can understand the bagging effect in terms of a consensus of independent weak learners. Let the Bayes optimal decision at x be G(x) = 1 in a two-class example. Suppose each of the weak learners G*b have an error-rate eb = e < 0.5, and let S1(x) = ∑ᴮb=1 I(G*b(x) = 1) be the consensus vote for class 1.

name: weak learners
best_quote: A weak classifier is one whose error rate is only slightly better than random guessing. The purpose of boosting is to sequentially apply the weak classification algorithm to repeatedly modified versions of the data

name: bayesian model averaging
best_quote: We have a set of candidate models Mm, m = 1, . . . , M for our training set Z. The posterior distribution of ζ is Pr(ζ|Z) = ∑ᴹm=1 Pr(ζ|Mm, Z)Pr(Mm|Z). This Bayesian prediction is a weighted average of the individual predictions, with weights proportional to the posterior probability of each model.

name: committee methods
best_quote: This formulation leads to a number of different model-averaging strategies. Committee methods take a simple unweighted average of the predictions from each model, essentially giving equal probability to each model.

name: stacking
best_quote: Stacked generalization, or stacking, is a way of doing this. Let f̂(−i)m(x) be the prediction at x, using model m, applied to the dataset with the ith training observation removed. The stacking estimate of the weights is obtained from the least squares linear regression of yi on f̂(−i)m(xi), m = 1, 2, . . . , M.

name: bumping
best_quote: Bumping uses bootstrap sampling to move randomly through model space. For problems where fitting method finds many local minima, bumping can help the method to avoid getting stuck in poor solutions. We draw bootstrap samples and fit a model to each. But rather than average the predictions, we choose the model estimated from a bootstrap sample that best fits the training data.

name: link function
best_quote: In general, the conditional mean μ(X) of a response Y is related to an additive function of the predictors via a link function g: g[μ(X)] = α + f1(X1) + · · · + fp(Xp).

name: backfitting algorithm
best_quote: We apply a cubic smoothing spline Sj to the targets {yi−α̂−∑k≠j f̂k(xik)}ᴺ₁, as a function of xij, to obtain a new estimate f̂j. This is done for each predictor in turn, using the current estimates of the other functions f̂k when computing yi−α̂−∑k≠j f̂k(xik). The process is continued until the estimates f̂j stabilize. This procedure is known as 'backfitting'.

name: pasting
best_quote: After the top-down sequence is computed, PRIM reverses the process, expanding along any edge, if such an expansion increases the box mean. This is called pasting. Since the top-down procedure is greedy at each step, such an expansion is often possible.

name: bump hunting
best_quote: The patient rule induction method (PRIM) also finds boxes in the feature space, but seeks boxes in which the response average is high. Hence it looks for maxima in the target function, an exercise known as bump hunting. (If minima rather than maxima are desired, one simply works with the negative response values.)

name: gating networks
best_quote: The terminal nodes are called experts, and the non-terminal nodes are called gating networks. The idea is that each expert provides an opinion (prediction) about the response, and these are combined together by the gating networks.

name: margin in classification
best_quote: In classification (with a -1/1 response) the margin plays a role analogous to the residuals y-f(x) in regression. The classification rule G(x) = sign[f(x)] implies that observations with positive margin yif(xi) > 0 are classified correctly whereas those with negative margin yif(xi) < 0 are misclassified.

name: generalized additive model
best_quote: In the regression setting, a generalized additive model has the form E(Y|X1, X2, . . ., Xp) = α + f1(X1) + f2(X2) + · · · + fp(Xp). As usual X1, X2, . . ., Xp represent predictors and Y is the outcome; the fj's are unspecified smooth ('nonparametric') functions.
formula: E(Y|X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_p) = \alpha + f_1(X_1) + f_2(X_2) + \cdots + f_p(X_p)

name: additive logistic regression
best_quote: The additive logistic regression model replaces each linear term by a more general functional form log[μ(X)/(1−μ(X))] = α + f1(X1) + · · · + fp(Xp) where again each fj is an unspecified smooth function.

name: identity link
best_quote: g(μ) = μ is the identity link, used for linear and additive models for Gaussian response data.

name: logit link
best_quote: g(μ) = logit(μ) as above, or g(μ) = probit(μ), the probit link function, for modeling binomial probabilities.

name: probit link
best_quote: The probit function is the inverse Gaussian cumulative distribution function: probit(μ) = Φ⁻¹(μ).

name: log link
best_quote: g(μ) = log(μ) for log-linear or log-additive models for Poisson count data.

name: local scoring algorithm
best_quote: For the logistic regression model and other generalized additive models, the appropriate criterion is a penalized log-likelihood. To maximize it, the backfitting procedure is used in conjunction with a likelihood maximizer. The Newton–Raphson routine for maximizing log-likelihoods can be recast as an IRLS (iteratively reweighted least squares) algorithm.

name: recursive binary partition
best_quote: To simplify matters, we restrict attention to recursive binary partitions like that in the top right panel of Figure 9.2. We first split the space into two regions, and model the response by the mean of Y in each region.

name: cost-complexity pruning
best_quote: The preferred strategy is to grow a large tree T0, stopping the splitting process only when some minimum node size (say 5) is reached. Then this large tree is pruned using cost-complexity pruning, which we now describe.

name: weakest link pruning
best_quote: To find Tα we use weakest link pruning: we successively collapse the internal node that produces the smallest per-node increase in Σ NmQm(T), and continue until we produce the single-node (root) tree.

name: node impurity
best_quote: Different measures Qm(T) of node impurity include the following: Misclassification error, Gini index, Cross-entropy or deviance

name: gini index
best_quote: Gini index: Σk≠k' p̂mk p̂mk' = ΣK p̂mk(1 - p̂mk).

name: cross-entropy for trees
best_quote: Cross-entropy or deviance: -ΣK p̂mk log p̂mk.

name: surrogate splits
best_quote: The second more general approach is the construction of surrogate variables. When considering a predictor for a split, we use only the observations for which that predictor is not missing. Having chosen the best (primary) predictor and split point, we form a list of surrogate predictors and split points.

name: sensitivity in classification
best_quote: Sensitivity: probability of predicting disease given true state is disease.

name: specificity in classification
best_quote: Specificity: probability of predicting non-disease given true state is non-disease.

name: receiver operating characteristic curve
best_quote: The receiver operating characteristic curve (ROC) is a commonly used summary for assessing the tradeoff between sensitivity and specificity. It is a plot of the sensitivity versus specificity as we vary the parameters of a classification rule.

name: c-statistic
best_quote: The area under the ROC curve, used above, is sometimes called the c-statistic. Interestingly, it can be shown that the area under the ROC curve is equivalent to the Mann-Whitney U statistic (or Wilcoxon rank-sum test), for the median difference between the prediction scores in the two groups

name: patient rule induction method
best_quote: The patient rule induction method (PRIM) also finds boxes in the feature space, but seeks boxes in which the response average is high. Hence it looks for maxima in the target function, an exercise known as bump hunting.

name: top-down peeling
best_quote: The main box construction method in PRIM works from the top down, starting with a box containing all of the data. The box is compressed along one face by a small amount, and the observations then falling outside the box are peeled off. The face chosen for compression is the one resulting in the largest box mean, after the compression is performed.

name: mars basis functions
best_quote: MARS uses expansions in piecewise linear basis functions of the form (x - t)+ and (t - x)+. The '+' means positive part, so (x-t)+ = x - t if x > t, 0 otherwise.

name: reflected pair
best_quote: Each function is piecewise linear, with a knot at the value t. In the terminology of Chapter 5, these are linear splines. We call the two functions a reflected pair in the discussion below.

name: generalized cross-validation for mars
best_quote: For computational savings the MARS procedure instead uses generalized cross-validation.

name: hierarchical mixtures of experts
best_quote: The hierarchical mixtures of experts (HME) procedure can be viewed as a variant of tree-based methods. The main difference is that the tree splits are not hard decisions but rather soft probabilistic ones. At each node an observation goes left or right with probabilities depending on its input values.

name: gating networks in hme
best_quote: The top gating network has the output gj(x, γj) = e^(γjᵀx) / ΣK e^(γkᵀx), j = 1, 2, ..., K, where each γj is a vector of unknown parameters. This represents a soft K-way split.

name: missing at random
best_quote: Data is said to be missing at random (MAR) if the distribution of R depends on the data Z only through Zobs: Pr(R|Z, θ) = Pr(R|Zobs, θ).

name: missing completely at random
best_quote: Data are said to be missing completely at random (MCAR) if the distribution of R doesn't depend on the observed or missing data: Pr(R|Z, θ) = Pr(R|θ).

name: weak classifier
best_quote: A weak classifier is one whose error rate is only slightly better than random guessing. The purpose of boosting is to sequentially apply the weak classification algorithm to repeatedly modified versions of the data

name: multinomial deviance
best_quote: The binomial deviance extends naturally to the K-class multinomial deviance loss function: -Σi Σk I(yi=Gk) log pk(xi).
